stories.headline: Elon Musk v. Sam Altman trial enters second week as safety record goes under the microscope
stories.summary: The federal trial in Oakland entered its second week with OpenAI president Greg Brockman and Shivon Zilis testifying about Musk's offers to Altman and the company's disputed history. Beneath the personal drama, witnesses have testified that product pressure compromised safety work — a detail receiving less coverage than the Musk-Altman relationship.
stories.channel_relevance_note: Every outlet is covering the Musk-Altman drama; almost nobody is covering what the testimony reveals about OpenAI's actual safety practices — that's where this channel lives.

stories.headline: Anthropic CEO warns of cybersecurity 'moment of danger' as Mythos model finds tens of thousands of software vulnerabilities
stories.summary: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei warned publicly that Mythos has found tens of thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities across every major OS and browser — nearly 300 in Firefox alone. Access is restricted to vetted partners under Project Glasswing. Amodei said the window to patch these vulnerabilities before bad actors find the same techniques is narrow.
stories.channel_relevance_note: A CEO publicly announcing his own product is dangerous enough to warrant restricted access — and framing it as an opportunity rather than an admission — deserves a closer read than most coverage gave it.

stories.headline: Anthropic signs deal to use all of xAI's Colossus 1 data center — months after Musk called Anthropic a civilization threat
stories.summary: Anthropic signed an agreement with SpaceX — which owns xAI — for all 300MW and 220,000 GPUs of the Colossus 1 Memphis data center. Musk had called Anthropic a company that 'hates Western civilization' in February, and is currently in trial against OpenAI. His firms are now compute suppliers to OpenAI's primary rival while he sues OpenAI.
stories.channel_relevance_note: Musk is simultaneously suing OpenAI for betraying AI safety, calling Anthropic a civilization threat, and signing compute deals that fund both — the gap between his stated principles and his business decisions is the whole story.

stories.headline: Google DeepMind UK workers vote 98% to unionize over Pentagon AI deal that overrides eight years of company ethics pledges
stories.summary: Employees at DeepMind's London offices voted 98% to form a union, directly citing Google's decision to let the Department of Defense use Gemini inside classified military networks 'for any lawful purpose.' Workers demand restoration of Google's scrapped weapons pledge, an independent ethics body, and the right to refuse projects that violate their ethics.
stories.channel_relevance_note: What broke the workers wasn't a product launch or a headline — it was a clause in a government contract saying 'any lawful purpose'; that detail explains why 98% crossed the line.

stories.headline: Meta to cut 8,000 jobs on May 20 as $135 billion AI spend reshapes workforce into AI-focused 'pods'
stories.summary: Meta will cut 8,000 employees on May 20 and cancel 6,000 open roles, simultaneously raising its 2026 AI capital expenditure guidance to $125–145 billion — up from $72 billion in 2025. Teams are being reorganised into AI-focused 'pods.' The framing from leadership is explicit: this is reallocation toward AI, not cost-cutting.
stories.channel_relevance_note: For years companies said AI would 'augment' workers; Meta is now saying 'we are replacing the humans with AI infrastructure' in plain language — and they're raising AI spending at the same time, not cutting costs.

stories.headline: Meta deploys AI system that scans users' physical characteristics to detect underage accounts — says it is not facial recognition
stories.summary: Meta will analyse height, bone structure, and other visual signals across all content types to identify users who may be under 13, then deactivate their accounts pending age verification. Meta insists this is not facial recognition. Critics raise questions about accuracy across demographic groups, consent for biometric profiling of all users (not just minors), and whether the system can actually be gamed.
stories.channel_relevance_note: The 'not facial recognition' line is doing a lot of work — biometric profiling of all users to find some users is a meaningful distinction that most coverage glossed over.

stories.headline: OpenAI finalizes $10 billion joint venture with 18 PE firms — embedding engineers inside businesses Palantir-style
stories.summary: OpenAI closed The Deployment Company, a $10 billion JV anchored by TPG with a guaranteed 17.5% annual return to PE investors over five years. The entity will embed OpenAI engineers directly inside client businesses in healthcare, shipping, manufacturing, and finance — a delivery pattern associated with Palantir. Anthropic announced a parallel $1.5 billion JV with Goldman Sachs and Blackstone within minutes.
stories.channel_relevance_note: OpenAI is effectively becoming a professional-services firm with a guaranteed PE return baked in — the Palantir comparison is the angle, and the Anthropic timing makes it a pattern story.

stories.headline: Microsoft weighing abandonment of 2030 carbon-free energy pledge as AI data centers add a gigawatt every three months
stories.summary: Bloomberg reports Microsoft is considering delaying or abandoning its commitment to match 100% of hourly electricity use with renewables by 2030. The company is adding one gigawatt of data center capacity every three months and faces insufficient renewable supply. Microsoft denies plans to abandon targets while simultaneously spending $190 billion on data centers — a figure that implies the climate pledges were made before AI economics were understood.
stories.channel_relevance_note: The story isn't 'AI is bad for the environment' — it's that every major AI company made climate pledges before anyone knew what running AI at scale would actually cost, and now the math doesn't work.

stories.headline: OpenAI releases GPT-5.5 Instant as new default ChatGPT model and claims 52% fewer hallucinations
stories.summary: OpenAI replaced GPT-5.3 Instant with GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT's default model, claiming internal evaluations show 52.5% fewer hallucinated claims on high-stakes medical, legal, and financial prompts. The hallucination benchmark was run by OpenAI itself — the same company whose product it evaluates.
stories.channel_relevance_note: 52% fewer hallucinations, per OpenAI — which is an interesting claim to make when you are the one who measured it on prompts you selected.

stories.headline: Anthropic launches $1.5 billion PE venture with Goldman Sachs and Blackstone — announced within minutes of OpenAI's parallel JV
stories.summary: Anthropic partnered with Goldman Sachs, Blackstone, and Hellman & Friedman to form a $1.5 billion joint venture targeting AI adoption in PE-owned companies — announced within minutes of OpenAI's $10 billion Deployment Company announcement. Both target the same sectors. The near-simultaneous timing is either competitive reaction, coordination, or a very striking coincidence.
stories.channel_relevance_note: Better covered as part of the OpenAI PE JV story — the parallel timing is the angle, not each deal individually.

stories.headline: US government signs deals to pre-test unreleased AI models from Google, Microsoft, and xAI — with no enforcement mechanism
stories.summary: NIST's Center for AI Standards signed agreements to evaluate pre-release models from Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI for security vulnerabilities before public deployment. The agreements are voluntary; the scope of 'security evaluation' is undefined in public documentation; there is no published enforcement mechanism if a model fails.
stories.channel_relevance_note: Voluntary government AI testing with no published enforcement criteria is the kind of governance-by-press-release that deserves exactly one sentence of scepticism.

stories.headline: Microsoft launches its first voluntary retirement program in 51 years as AI infrastructure spending tightens other budgets
stories.summary: Microsoft offered approximately 8,750 U.S. employees a voluntary buyout with a June 8 deadline — its first such programme in 51 years. The move comes alongside accelerating AI capex and reported budget cuts in non-AI divisions, suggesting the company is internally redistributing resources toward AI at the expense of other functions.
stories.channel_relevance_note: Best used as a data point in a broader 'how AI is actually reshaping tech employment' story rather than as a standalone.

stories.headline: Federal court pauses Colorado's AI Act weeks before its effective date; DOJ argues the law compels AI to adopt state viewpoints
stories.summary: A federal court stayed Colorado SB 24-205 on April 27, blocking investigations until the case is resolved. xAI filed the constitutional challenge. The US Department of Justice intervened to argue the law 'impermissibly compels AI systems to adopt state-defined viewpoints' — a legal framing that, if it holds, could be used to block most AI regulation at state level.
stories.channel_relevance_note: The DOJ's specific argument — that regulating AI outputs is compelled speech — is a legal weapon that could be deployed against essentially any state AI regulation; this is buried in legal press and nobody is explaining what it actually means.

stories.headline: Researchers expose 15,500-domain AI-themed deepfake investment fraud network using ad trackers as cloaking infrastructure
stories.summary: Malwarebytes documented an AI investment fraud operation spanning 15,500+ domains that uses the Keitaro ad-tracking platform to show deepfake celebrity endorsements to real victims while displaying harmless content to security scanners. Generative AI produces multilingual campaign material at scale. Domain takedowns have had minimal effect — operators rotate infrastructure faster than it can be removed.
stories.channel_relevance_note: The mechanism — using legitimate ad infrastructure to hide scam content from the scanners — is both technically interesting and a story about how AI scam operations now operate at industrial scale.

stories.headline: OpenAI rolls out GPT-5.5-Cyber to restricted security teams days after Anthropic warned about AI-enabled vulnerability discovery
stories.summary: OpenAI began distributing GPT-5.5-Cyber to vetted cybersecurity teams in limited preview. The release follows Anthropic's Mythos warning by days. Both labs are independently building specialised security models while restricting access — suggesting a pattern of frontier labs racing into cyberdefence while treating their own most capable models as too dangerous for open access.
stories.channel_relevance_note: Only useful as a supporting data point in the Mythos story — not strong enough standalone, but adds to the pattern of both labs restricting their most security-capable models.

stories.headline: OpenAI expects to spend $50 billion on computing in 2026
stories.summary: OpenAI president Greg Brockman disclosed the company expects to spend $50 billion on computing in 2026, up from roughly $30 million in 2017. The figure is striking but is primarily a disclosure data point without a specific investigative angle beyond scale.
stories.channel_relevance_note: A useful supporting statistic but not a channel story on its own.
